p.strong: Tied-Array Beams for Pulsar Search and Timing (PST)

ac:rich-text-body.p: Tied-array beams coherently combine signals from multiple antennas or stations across the SKA to achieve maximum sensitivity to point sources. The PST (Pulsar Search and Timing) backend processes these beams in real-time for pulsar detection and millisecond-precision timing.

p: A tied-array beam is formed by coherently combining (co-adding with proper phase alignment) the voltage signals from many antennas or antenna groups. Unlike correlation or imaging beams, which operate on visibilities (cross-correlations), tied-array beams preserve the full signal phase and amplitude, enabling:

ul.li: Maximum point-source sensitivity: All collecting area is incoherently added, yielding sensitivity approaching N times the single-antenna sensitivity (where N is the number of elements)
Narrow instantaneous field of view: Coherent phasing creates a sharp, directed beam (~1–10 arcmin depending on frequency and frequency resolution)
Real-time signal inspection: The high signal-to-noise ratio enables immediate detection of pulsars, transients, and other signals

p: This is distinct from an imaging (correlation) beam, which creates full-resolution images by cross-correlating all pairs of antennas. Tied-array beams sacrifice spatial information for temporal resolution and sensitivity.

tr.td: Wide (variable, from ~1 degree to several degrees)
Very low (single time series per beam)
High but limited by image noise

ol.li: Antenna/station selection: Choose which array elements (individual antennas for SKA-Mid, stations for SKA-Low, or substations within SKA-Low) will contribute to the beam
Voltage digitization: Each antenna's voltage signal is digitized at the receiver (e.g., 8-bit sampling at the Nyquist frequency)
Channelization: The full bandwidth is divided into narrow frequency channels (typically 1 kHz to 1 MHz depending on required spectral resolution)

Delay correction: Geometric delays (due to array geometry and Earth rotation) are corrected in real-time. Each antenna's signal is shifted by the appropriate delay to align signals from a chosen sky position.
Phase correction: Additional phase shifts compensate for instrumental phase errors and, for low frequencies, ionospheric delays
Coherent co-addition: All corrected voltage signals are summed with proper complex weighting, forming a single complex time series per beam
Backend processing: The PST backend processes the coherent beam(s) for pulsar search, timing, or other time-domain science

p: The Pulsar Search and Timing (PST) backend processes tied-array beams in real-time:

ul.li: Dedispersion: Removes the frequency-dependent delay caused by propagation through the ionosphere and interstellar medium, allowing coherent addition across frequency channels
Folding: Optionally folds data on known pulsar periods for sensitivity enhancement
Detection statistics: Computes signal-to-noise ratios and thresholds for automatic detection of new pulsars
Output: Candidate list with pulse properties (period, DM, significance)
High time resolution: Records voltages or search-mode output with µs-to-ms time resolution for precision timing
Multi-epoch observations: Supports repeated observations of known pulsars over weeks to years for gravitational-wave detection and tests of relativity
Polarimetry: Measures linear and circular polarization of pulsar signals (SKA-Mid capability)
Timing residuals: Provides timing residuals (microsecond-precision) for pulsar-timing-array analysis
Real-time filtering: Searches for bursts, flares, and other transient signals exceeding user-defined thresholds
Fast alert generation: Outputs candidate events within seconds for rapid follow-up by other facilities

h2: Beam Sensitivity and Field of View

ul.li: Tied-array beams achieve the theoretical limit: SEFD = SEFD_single / sqrt(N_elements)
For SKA-Low with 512 stations, a full-array tied-array beam reaches ~0.5–1 mJy (at mid-frequencies), making it sensitive to even weak pulsars
Partial-array beams (fewer stations) have proportionally higher SEFD but are quicker to reconfigure and less computationally expensive
The coherence bandwidth (frequency channels that remain coherent after delay correction) defines the beam width: θ ~ λ / B, where B is the baseline length
SKA-Low full-array: IFOV ~1–10 arcmin (depends on frequency)
SKA-Mid: IFOV typically a few arcsec (limited by longest baselines and frequency)
Only one position in the sky can be observed per tied-array beam at a time (unlike imaging mode)

p: The number of simultaneous tied-array beams is limited by:

ul.li: Computational capacity: Each beam requires real-time FFT, dedispersion, and folding. Typical back-end hardware supports ~1–10 simultaneous beams depending on spectral resolution and data rate.
Phasing infrastructure: Forming multiple tied-array beams from the same antenna set requires multiple phase correction paths, limited by available correlator/beamformer resources
Data rate: Each beam generates a data stream of ~MB/s; total data rate must stay within storage and network capacity

ac:rich-text-body.p: During early cycles, expect to have ~1–4 simultaneous tied-array beams available. The SKA operations team will provide actual limits based on operational experience and back-end utilization. Proposals should specify whether they require multiple simultaneous beams and the motivation.

p: Tied-array beams can operate as primary observing mode (dedicated telescope time) or as commensals on an imaging observation. In commensal mode:

ul.li: The host provides a continuum imaging observation, feeding data to the PST backend
The PST backend forms one or more tied-array beams within the imaging field of view, processing them for pulsar search/timing
No additional antenna time is consumed; the commensal beam shares correlator and back-end resources with the host
This enables efficient simultaneous continuum imaging and pulsar monitoring

p: The SKA can discover thousands of new pulsars through sensitive surveys at SKA-Low frequencies. Tied-array beams enable:

ul.li: Discovery of millisecond pulsars in dense stellar environments
Detection of recycled pulsars in globular clusters
Identification of pulsar populations at intermediate distances (kpc–Mpc scale)

p: Arrays of precisely timed pulsars are sensitive probes of gravitational-wave backgrounds and tests of fundamental physics. SKA-based pulsar timing will:

ul.li: Achieve microsecond timing precision (improving on current by factor of 10+)
Enable detection of gravitational waves from supermassive black hole mergers
Test equivalence principle and strong-field gravity
Pulsar magnetar outbursts and glitches
Pulsar nulling and mode changes
RFI and solar bursts (for characterization)
Rapid polarization changes during pulsar flares

h2: Requesting Tied-Array Beams in Your Proposal

ul.li: Target list: Pulsar names or sky coordinates
Observation mode: Search or timing
Frequency band(s): SKA-Low, SKA-Mid, or both
Dispersion measure range: For search mode (helps optimize dedispersion)
Timing precision requirement: For timing mode (drives frequency resolution and coherence time)
Number of simultaneous beams: If observing multiple targets concurrently
Observing frequency: Annual cadence for pulsar-timing-array targets

p: The SKA User Support team will advise on feasibility and expected sensitivity during proposal review.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Detailed specifications for SKA pulsar capabilities are available in the SKA Pulsar Science Book and PST Backend Technical Specifications documents. For questions specific to your science case, contact the SKA Pulsar Science Working Group via the User Support portal.
